source_text: Tomorrow I will speak with the director.

reference_translations.notes: Standard. Subject pronoun dropped, the natural default.
WRONG: keeps the -are a instead of shifting to e. Guards against the commonest regular-future slip.

explanation: Parlare is a regular -are verb, so its future shifts the theme vowel a to e: parler- plus the io ending -ò gives parlerò. 'Domani' (tomorrow) fixes the future. Italian normally drops the subject pronoun, so parlerò alone already means 'I will speak'.

source_text: Next year they will work in the city centre.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: L'anno prossimo loro lavoreranno in centro.
reference_translations.notes: Subject pronoun loro included for emphasis.
WRONG: single n in the 3pl ending; it must be -anno.

explanation: Lavorare shifts a to e and takes the loro ending -anno with its double n: lavoreranno. 'L'anno prossimo' (next year) fixes the future.

source_text: I will take the nine o'clock train.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: prendere is regular in the future and must not be syncopated; the e stays.

explanation: A regular -ere verb keeps its theme vowel e (no shift) and adds -ò: prenderò. The contraction prendrò belongs only to the irregular syncopated set (avrò, vedrò), not to a regular -ere verb.

source_text: She will write you a letter.

reference_translations.notes: Standard. Subject pronoun dropped.
Subject pronoun lei included.

explanation: Scrivere is regular in the future: the e stays and the 3sg ending -à joins on, giving scriverà. The accent on -à is obligatory. Don't syncopate it (scrivrà).

source_text: With time you will understand everything.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: the present's -isc- insert does not appear in the future.

explanation: Capire takes -isc- in the present (capisco) but never in the future: the stem is simply capir-, giving capirai for tu. Carrying the insert across (capiscirai) is the slip to avoid.

source_text: Tomorrow I will look for a new job.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: -care needs an h before the future -er- to keep the hard c.

translation.reveal: to look for / to search - cercare

explanation: Cercare ends in -care, where the c is hard. The future ending starts with e, so an h is inserted to keep the hard sound: cercherò. Without the h (cercerò) the c would soften.

source_text: Next summer we will travel to Asia.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: -giare drops the redundant i before the future -er-.

explanation: Viaggiare ends in -giare; the i only softened the g in the infinitive, and the future e does that job, so the i is dropped: viaggeremo. Keeping it (viaggieremo) is the typical miss.

source_text: Tomorrow I will have more free time.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: avere syncopates to avr-; the theme vowel drops.

explanation: Avere is syncopated in the future: the theme vowel drops, giving the stem avr- and then -ò: avrò. Keeping the vowel (averò) is the slip. This same avrò is also the auxiliary for the futuro anteriore.

source_text: They will not be able to come.

reference_translations.notes: Standard. potere syncopated (potr-) + venire double-r is incidental here.
WRONG: potere syncopates to potr-; the theme vowel drops.

translation.reveal: to be able to / can - potere

explanation: Potere syncopates to the stem potr- and takes the loro ending -anno: potranno. The infinitive venire that follows stays in its dictionary form, so only potere is conjugated here.
examiner_note: The required skill is potere's syncope; venire's double-r future is optional (it appears only if the learner expands the infinitive, which they should not).

source_text: We will have to leave early.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: dovere syncopates to dovr-; the theme vowel drops.

explanation: Dovere syncopates to the stem dovr- and takes the noi ending -emo: dovremo. The infinitive partire that follows is not conjugated.

source_text: She will come to the party with us.

reference_translations.notes: Standard. Subject pronoun dropped.
WRONG: venire assimilates to verr-; a single r is the classic slip.

explanation: Venire is a double-r verb in the future: the stem assimilates to verr- and takes -à, giving verrà. The doubled r is the signature; a single r (verà) is the miss. The accent on -à is obligatory. Note 'alla' = a + la.

source_text: I will want to know the whole truth.

reference_translations.notes: Standard.
WRONG: volere assimilates to vorr-; a single r is the slip.

explanation: Volere is a double-r verb: the stem assimilates to vorr- and takes -ò, giving vorrò. The infinitive sapere that follows is not conjugated. The doubled r is the whole point; vorò is the miss.

source_text: Tomorrow I will be at home all day.

reference_translations.text: Domani sarò a casa tutto il giorno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Domani esserò a casa tutto il giorno.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: essere uses the suppletive stem sar-, not a stem built from the infinitive.

explanation: Essere has the suppletive future stem sar-, which you cannot derive from the infinitive, then the regular endings: sarò. Building from the infinitive (esserò) is the natural but wrong move. The accent on -ò is obligatory.

source_text: The children will be tired after the trip.

reference_translations.text: I bambini saranno stanchi dopo il viaggio.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. stanchi is the masculine plural of stanco (note the h).

reference_translations.text: I bambini esseranno stanchi dopo il viaggio.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: essere uses the suppletive stem sar-.

explanation: I bambini is they, so essere's suppletive stem sar- takes the loro ending -anno: saranno. The infinitive-built esseranno is the slip.

source_text: Tomorrow I will do my homework.

reference_translations.notes: Standard. Italian uses the definite article i, not a possessive, for one's own homework.
WRONG: fare keeps -ar- and does not take the -are a-to-e shift.

explanation: Fare keeps -ar- in the future, against the usual -are shift: far- plus -ò gives farò. Applying the shift (ferò) over-generalises the regular rule. Contrast with parlerò, where the shift IS required.

source_text: She will give you a hand with the move.

reference_translations.text: Ti darà una mano con il trasloco.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'dare una mano' = to give a hand / to help.

reference_translations.text: Ti derà una mano con il trasloco.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: dare keeps -ar- and does not take the -are a-to-e shift.

explanation: Dare keeps -ar- against the usual -are shift: dar- plus -à gives darà. Shifting the vowel (derà) treats dare like an ordinary -are verb, which here it is not. The accent on -à is obligatory.

source_text: When you arrive, I will have already finished.

reference_translations.notes: Standard. Subject pronoun dropped.
WRONG: the simple future says 'I will finish', not 'I will have finished'; the action completed before another future point needs the futuro anteriore.

explanation: The action finished before another future moment is the futuro anteriore: the future of avere (avrò) plus the participle finito gives avrò finito. The subordinate clause uses the simple future (arriverai). Two future tenses sit side by side: the simple future for the later event, the compound for the one already over.
examiner_note: arrivare's simple future in the 'quando' clause is optional; the required skill is the futuro anteriore.

source_text: When you come back, Maria will have already left.

reference_translations.text: Quando tornerai, Maria sarà già partita.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. essere auxiliary, participle agrees with feminine singular Maria.

reference_translations.text: Quando tornerai, Maria sarà già partito.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: with essere the participle must agree with the feminine subject (partita, not partito).

reference_translations.text: Quando tornerai, Maria avrà già partito.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: partire is a motion verb and takes essere, not avere.

translation.reveal: to come back / to return - tornare

explanation: The futuro anteriore of partire uses essere (a motion verb): the future of essere (sarà) plus the participle gives the compound. With essere the participle agrees with the subject, and Maria is feminine singular, so it is sarà partita. Using avere (avrà partito) or a non-agreeing participle (sarà partito) are the two misses.

source_text: By tomorrow morning the girls will have arrived.

reference_translations.text: Per domani mattina le ragazze saranno arrivate.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. essere auxiliary, participle agrees with feminine plural le ragazze.

reference_translations.text: Per domani mattina le ragazze saranno arrivati.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the participle must agree feminine plural (arrivate, not arrivati).

explanation: The futuro anteriore of arrivare uses essere: the future of essere (saranno) plus the participle gives the compound. With essere the participle agrees with the subject, and le ragazze is feminine plural, so it is saranno arrivate.
